title: Pre-Existing Violator Discovery and Operator Triage Dashboard

story.as_a: tenant operator rolling out the Chain of Custody v2 invariants to an existing deployment
story.i_want_to: see a dashboard of pre-existing data that violates the new rules (cross-role agent bindings, nil-agent non-user keys, review records with nil reviewer, orphaned audit entries) with actionable remediation per row
story.so_that: I can triage and resolve violations manually before the hard cutover without the system auto-deleting anything I might still need

rationale: Phase 1 ships new invariants that block future violations, but existing data that predates the rules is grandfathered in. Silently ignoring pre-existing violations creates false confidence — the operator will believe the system is protected when some tenants still hold exploit vectors. This story ships a read-only discovery pass + an operator-facing dashboard to surface every violation with context and remediation options. Resolution is always operator-driven, never automated.

acceptance_criteria.description: Mix task `mix loopctl.audit.discover_violators` runs a series of read-only queries and prints a report to stdout with counts per violation type. Exit code is 0 if no violations, 1 if any exist.

Violation types detected: (a) cross-role agent binding — same (tenant_id, agent_id) has multiple active keys with different roles; (b) nil-agent non-user key — api_key with role != user AND agent_id IS NULL; (c) api_key referencing a non-existent agent — FK would fail; (d) review_records with reviewer_agent_id IS NULL; (e) audit entries (if any exist pre-migration) without a chain hash; (f) stories in reported_done that never had a corresponding review record.

Each detected violation writes a row to a new `audit_pending_violations` table: id (uuid), tenant_id, violation_type (enum), entity_type, entity_id, discovered_at, detail (jsonb), status (enum: pending/resolved/ignored), resolved_at, resolved_by_api_key_id, resolution_note.
`GET /api/v1/admin/violators` returns all pending violations as JSON. Requires role `:superadmin`. Paginated with filters by tenant_id and violation_type.

LiveView at `/admin/violators` (requires superadmin key via the existing admin live_session) displays the violation list grouped by violation_type. Each row has metadata (entity, discovered_at, detail) and action buttons: "Revoke key" (for key-based violations), "Reject story" (for story-based), "Ignore" (for manual review), "Mark resolved" (for manually-fixed).

Every action from the dashboard is logged in the audit chain with action `violation_resolved` or `violation_ignored`, actor_lineage of the operator, and detail including the original violation and the resolution note.

The dashboard shows a prominent summary: total violations, count per type, and a "cutover countdown" (days remaining until the hard cutover enforces the new rules strictly). The cutover date is configured in `config/config.exs` as a fixed timestamp.
The dashboard follows `docs/design-system.md` — dark mode, slate palette, rounded-md cards, terminal aesthetic. Action buttons use the existing `.button` component with destructive/primary variants.
Tenant isolation: operators with a tenant-scoped admin key see only their tenant's violations. Loopctl-root superadmin key sees all tenants.

System article at slug `pre-existing-violators` documents the triage process: what each violation type means, the recommended remediation, and what happens if a violation goes unresolved past the cutover date. Article is authored inline in this story (short — ~500-800 words).
CHANGELOG entry describes the discovery mechanism and the dashboard. Deployment notes explain how to configure the cutover date and how to interpret the violation counts.

test_cases.name: Mix task discovers cross-role bindings and writes pending violation rows
preconditions: Bypass the new unique index to insert two active keys for the same agent with different roles (use raw SQL or AdminRepo)
expected_results: audit_pending_violations has 1 row of type cross_role_binding

test_cases.name: Dashboard lists violations with actions
preconditions: audit_pending_violations has 3 rows of different types
expected_results: Page shows 3 violations grouped by type

test_cases.name: Resolving a violation writes an audit chain entry
expected_results: Audit chain has a new entry with action = violation_resolved
Payload includes the violation detail and the note

test_cases.name: Tenant-scoped admin sees only their tenant's violations
preconditions: tenant-admin key for tenant_a (user role, scoped)

technical_notes: The task uses AdminRepo (BYPASSRLS) to read across all tenants for a superadmin-run scan.

Each violation type corresponds to a specific SQL query. Example for cross-role: `SELECT tenant_id, agent_id, array_agg(DISTINCT role) FROM api_keys WHERE revoked_at IS NULL AND agent_id IS NOT NULL GROUP BY tenant_id, agent_id HAVING count(DISTINCT role) > 1`
Migration for audit_pending_violations: standard fields + jsonb detail + enum status.
LiveView: lib/loopctl_web/live/admin/violators_live.ex in the existing admin live_session.
Action buttons: "Revoke key" calls Loopctl.Auth.revoke_api_key via the existing function. "Reject story" calls Progress.reject_story. Both write to audit_chain automatically.
The wiki article is authored as a heredoc in the seed migration, matching the pattern from US-26.0.5.
